sanskrit_devanagari:
परस्तस्मात्तु भावोऽन्योऽव्यक्तोऽव्यक्तात्सनातनः ।
यः स सर्वेषु भूतेषु नश्यत्सु न विनश्यति ॥ २० ॥
sanskrit_iast:
paras tasmāt tu bhāvo 'nyo 'vyakto 'vyaktāt sanātanaḥ
yaḥ sa sarveṣu bhūteṣu naśyatsu na vinaśyati || 20 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (canonical body text; vedabase concatenates the two halves; danda restored from gitasupersite)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।8.20।।)
exact IAST (verbatim from page; sandhi conventions match GRETIL)

translations.verbatim_quote: Yet there is another unmanifest nature, which is eternal and is transcendental to this manifested and unmanifested matter. It is supreme and is never annihilated. When all in this world is annihilated, that part remains as it is.
Yet, there is another unmanifest, which is eternal and is transcendental to the manifest and the unmanifest. That Supreme Divine Personality does not perish even when all beings perish.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Kṛṣṇa’s superior, spiritual energy is transcendental and eternal. It is beyond all the changes of material nature, which is manifest and annihilated during the days and nights of Brahmā. Kṛṣṇa’s superior energy is completely opposite in quality to material nature.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Prabhupada glosses BG 8.20 in the Gaudiya Vaishnava key.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.20 He is parah, distinct, different;-From what?-tasmat, from that aforesaid (Unmanifested). The word tu, but, is meant for showing the distinction of the Immutable that is going to be spoken of from the Unmanifested. He is bhavah, the Reality, the supreme Brahman called the Immutable. Ev…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads BG 8.20 in the Advaita non-dual key.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.20 – 8.21 Superior, as an object of human end, to this unmanifest (Avyakta), which is inanimate Prakrti, there is another state of being, of a kind different from this, but also called Avyakta. It has only knowledge-form and is also unmanifest. It is the self, Atman. It is unmanifest bec…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads BG 8.20 in the Vishishtadvaita devotional-qualified key.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: After completing the expose on the impermanence of all material worlds, Lord Krishna elucidates in this verse and the next on the eternal nature of the spiritual worlds which exist beyond avyakta the unmanifest and which is never destroyed when all the material worlds perish. Beyond the av…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Sridhara provides a synthetic gloss bridging Advaita and devotional readings.

positions.rendering: another unmanifest nature ... eternal and transcendental to this manifested and unmanifested matter
another unmanifest ... eternal and transcendental to the manifest and the unmanifest
the supreme Brahman, the Immutable, distinct from the unmanifest characterised as ignorance (avidyā)
the self (ātman) of knowledge-form, distinct from inanimate prakṛti

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The verse names a higher unmanifest (avyakta) beyond the standard prakṛti unmanifest, and that higher unmanifest is sanātana, eternal. The traditions diverge sharply on its identity. Advaita (Shankara) reads it as the supreme Brahman, the Immutable, contrasted with the unmanifest-as-ignorance. Vishishtadvaita (Ramanuja) reads it as the self/ātman in its essential nature. Modern translations preserve the literal Sanskrit shape (an unmanifest beyond the unmanifest) without committing to a metaphysics. For an engineering layer this is a hard scope-mismatch: the higher unmanifest is irreducibly metaphysical.

literal_meaning: But beyond that unmanifest there is another unmanifest, eternal — that which, when all beings perish, does not perish.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna pivots from cosmic-cycles teaching (8.17-8.19) to the doctrine of the higher unmanifest. Beyond the avyakta-prakṛti (the unmanifest as the seed of beings) is another avyakta — eternal, sanātana, that does not perish when all beings perish in the cosmic dissolution. Shankara identifies this as the supreme Brahman, the Immutable beyond ignorance. Ramanuja identifies it as the self (ātman) in its essential nature, distinct from inanimate prakṛti. Sridhara reads it as the cause of prakṛti itself. All traditions agree: the higher unmanifest is metaphysically distinct from the lower and eternal across cosmic dissolutions.

remaining_gaps: Madhva did not comment on BG 8.20 (per Sarvabhashya tradition).
Ramanuja verbatim — page renders commentary in JS; bhagavad-gita.us static-HTML excerpt captured but full Adidevananda edition would deepen the citation.
